transcript: Welcome to Lesson 7.8, Multi-Channel Workflows Combining Email and SMS. Over the past five lessons, you have built workflows that send emails, fire SMS messages, book appointments, follow up on abandoned offers, and organize contacts through tags and pipelines. Each of those workflows use channels, but mostly in isolation. This lesson teaches you how to combine email and SMS into one coordinated intelligent sequence that reaches every contact through the channel they actually respond to. By the end of this lesson, you will understand why multi-channel outperforms single channel with real data behind it. What email and SMS each do best and where each falls short? How to assign the right channel to every touchpoint in a sequence? How to design a complete multi-channel workflow with deliberate ordering and timing? How to use behavior-based conditions to switch channels when engagement drops? And what compliance requirements govern both channels? Let's get into it. Let's start with the business case, because the numbers here are impossible to ignore. The average email open rate across industries is 20-35%. That means if you are running email-only sequences, 65-80% of your contact list never sees your message. Those contacts may be genuinely interested in the offer. They may be perfectly qualified prospects. But if they prefer SMS over email, or if they simply miss the email in an overflowing inbox, they will never hear from you, and the opportunity is permanently lost. Adding SMS to the sequence changes the equation fundamentally. SMS carries a 98% open rate and is read within 3 minutes of delivery on average. While your email is competing with dozens of other messages in an inbox, your SMS arrives in the same thread as messages from friends and family. The attention is immediate and personal. Here is the statistic that makes the business case definitively. Multi-channel campaigns generate 287% higher purchase rates than single-channel campaigns. That is not a marginal improvement. That is a transformational difference in results from the same leads you already have. The goal is not to overwhelm contacts with messages from two directions simultaneously. The goal is to find the channel each contact responds to and use it intentionally at the right moment. That is what a well-designed multi-channel workflow does. Before you can design a multi-channel sequence, you need to understand exactly what each channel does best, because using the wrong channel at the wrong moment undermines the entire sequence. Email excels at depth. It gives you unlimited space for storytelling, education, and detailed offer presentation. You can include images, branded templates, formatted layouts, and multiple links. The contact reads it at their own pace with no pressure. A well-written email can educate, overcome objections, and build trust in a single message. Email is also virtually cost-free at scale, making it the default channel for high-volume nurture sequences. Email's limitation is immediacy. An email can sit unread for hours or days. In a competitive inbox, even a great subject line may not earn an open. And for contacts who primarily communicate by text rather than email, your message may never truly land regardless of how well it is written. SMS excels at immediacy and personal presence. With a 98% open rate and a 3-minute average read time, SMS is the highest immediacy channel available in Go High Level. It arrives in the same inbox as personal conversations, which makes it feel direct and important. It forces clarity. You have 160 characters to make one specific point and one specific ask. The response rate to SMS calls to action is significantly higher than email for time-sensitive moments. SMS's limitation is depth. You cannot educate, tell a story, or present a complex offer in 160 characters. You cannot include rich media or branded formatting. An SMS requires explicit written opt-in consent, strict compliance requirements that must be in place before a single message is sent. The guiding principle is simple. Email educates and nurtures. SMS activates and reminds. Neither replaces the other. They play different roles in the same sequence. Now let me give you a practical framework for deciding which channel to use at each touchpoint. Because the most common multi mistake is adding channels randomly rather than assigning them deliberately Start with two questions First, does this message need to be read immediately? If yes, urgency is a factor. SMS belongs in this touchpoint, either alone or alongside email. If no, timing is flexible, email is the right channel. Second, does this message require explanation, context, or detail? If yes, email must be the primary channel. SMS can support it, but the substance of the message needs space that only email provides. If no, if this is a short direct prompt, SMS alone may be sufficient, or SMS reinforcing an email already sent. Let me show you how this plays out across common touchpoint types. A welcome email with lead magnet delivery needs warmth, detail, and a download link. That is email only. SMS is too personal too soon in the relationship. Educational nurture content needs space for storytelling and teaching. That is email. An appointment reminder 24 hours before needs maximum show-up rate. That is both email and SMS together. An appointment reminder one hour before needs immediate attention only. That is SMS alone. No email needed at that point. A final offer deadline needs both channels. The full message goes by email and SMS delivers an urgent nudge two hours later to drive the email open. Re-engaging a contact who has stopped opening emails. That calls for shifting SMS to the primary channel entirely. The rule that governs all of this, default to email. Add SMS when urgency matters, when a reminder needs immediate attention, or when a contact has stopped responding to email. Every channel in the sequence must have a reason for being there. Now let me show you how to design a complete multi-channel sequence using deliberate channel ordering and timing. I want to walk you through a five-day lead nurture sequence as a real design example because this is the pattern you will use most frequently. Day zero, immediately after the trigger fires, send the welcome email. The relationship is brand new. Email is the right channel at this moment. It allows warmth, detail, and the actual delivery of whatever the contact signed up for. SMS at this point would feel intrusive before any relationship exists. Day 1. Send a value email. Educational content, no offer, pure credibility building. Still email only. The contact is in the early orientation stage and needs depth, not urgency. Day 2. Send the first SMS. This is a strategic channel shift. After two days of email contact, the contact may be engaged or they may not have opened either email. The SMS serves two purposes simultaneously. It re-engages the attention of non-openers through a different channel, and it adds a personal touch for the contacts who did open and engage. The SMS content is conversational. Did you get a chance to read the guide? Here is a quick tip related to what you downloaded. Short, personal, and links to something genuinely helpful. Day 3 Send the trust email. A case study or testimonial featuring someone like the target persona with a specific result. Back to email. This message needs space for a story. The day 2 SMS likely drove some additional email opens, so this day 3 email now reaches a warmer audience. Day 5. This is the soft offer touchpoint, and this is where both channels fire together deliberately. Send the offer email first with the full benefit presentation and a clear call to action. Then, two hours later, send an SMS, not to repeat the offer, but to drive the email open. The SMS says something like, hi, first name, just sent you something relevant to their goal, worth a quick look. The SMS is not the offer. It is the trigger that gets the offer read. This two-hour delay between email and SMS on the same day is a critical design principle. Sending both simultaneously creates a synchronized blast that feels coordinated poorly. Sending the SMS two hours after the email uses SMS exactly as it should be used to amplify email open rates at a specific high-value moment. Here is what this complete multi-channel sequence looks like inside GoHighLevel's workflow builder. You can see the channels alternating deliberately. Email blocks carry the depth and the substance SMS blocks arrive between emails to re and amplify Notice the IF slash ELSE condition block before the day 5 offer touchpoint It checks whether the contact has opened any previous email in the sequence. Contacts in the YES branch receive the offer email first, followed by the SMS nudge two hours later. Contacts in the NO branch who have not opened a single email in five days, go directly to SMS as the primary channel with a completely different angle. This is behavior-based channel switching in action, and you can see how it is built inside the same workflow canvas you have been using since lesson 7.3. Let me go deeper on behavior-based channel switching because this is what separates a truly intelligent multi-channel workflow from a sequence that just randomly fires emails and texts. The problem it solves is this. A contact has received four emails in your sequence and opened none of them. Without any switching logic, the sequence continues delivering emails they will never read. The relationship quietly dies. Meanwhile, this contact might be highly engaged on SMS. They just happen to be someone who responds to texts. not emails. Behavior-based channel switching detects this pattern and automatically shifts to SMS as the primary channel instead of continuing to send ignored emails. There are three switching patterns you will use most frequently. Pattern number one is the email to SMS switch. When a contact has not opened the last two emails, an if-slash-else condition detects the non-engagement and routes them to an SMS with a fresh angle. Not the same message sent by text, but a new hook that gives them a reason to engage. Pattern number two is SMS drives email open. Instead of switching channels, this pattern uses SMS to amplify email performance. After sending an important email, a two-hour wait is followed by an if-slash-else check. Did they open it? If no, fire an SMS nudging them to check their inbox. This pattern is particularly effective for offer emails and final push touchpoints. Pattern number three is convert removes from sequence. When a contact clicks the offer link, an if slash else condition detects the conversion intent and removes them from the nurture workflow immediately. Continuing to send nurture content to someone who just expressed purchase intent is one of the most damaging multi-channel mistakes. It undercuts the momentum of their decision. The principle behind all three patterns is identical. A system that adapts to contact behavior outperforms a system that sends the same sequence to everyone regardless of what they actually do. Conditions are what make multi-channel workflows intelligent rather than mechanical. Before you activate any multi-channel workflow, you must understand the compliance requirements for both channels. This is not optional background information. These are legal obligations with real financial consequences. For email, the governing regulations in the United States are the CanSpam Act and internationally the GDPR for European contacts. The requirements are Honest subject lines with no deceptive framing Clear sender identification So contacts know who sent the email A physical mailing address in every commercial email A visible and functional unsubscribe link and honoring unsubscribe requests promptly. GoHighLevel automatically handles unsubscribe links and sender information when your email sending domain is properly configured. But verify these settings before sending. For SMS, the governing regulation in the United States is the Telephone Consumer Protection Act, known as TCPA, and internationally the GDPR. The requirements are significantly stricter than email. You must have explicit written opt-in before sending any marketing SMS. Verbal consent is not sufficient. The opt-in process must clearly disclose that the contact is agreeing to receive text messages from your business. Every SMS must identify your business. Every SMS must include opt-out instructions such as reply stop to unsubscribe. Opt-out requests must be honored immediately. And no SMS should be sent before 8 a.m. or after 9 p.m. in the contact's local time zone. Here is the financial reality of non-compliance. Fines under TCPA can reach $1,500 per unsolicited text message. These are not theoretical penalties. Businesses are fined regularly for SMS compliance violations. Before any contact enters an SMS workflow in GoHighLevel, their opt-in status must be verified. Building an if condition at the start of any SMS containing workflow to check for an SMS opt tag is a professional standard you should apply to every system you build Let me close with the seven mistakes that most commonly damage multi-channel engagement before you activate any workflow. Mistake number one, sending SMS and email with the same message at the same time. Each channel carries a different message. SMS reinforces or drives action on the email. Sending both simultaneously with the same content makes the sequence feel like spam from two directions. Mistake number two, adding SMS to a workflow without verifying opt-in. Build a condition at the top of every SMS containing workflow that checks for an SMS consent tag. Any contact without that tag should exit the workflow or be routed to email only. Mistake number three, sending SMS at the wrong time of day. Configure send time windows in GHL to restrict SMS delivery to 9 a.m. to 8 p.m. in the contact's local time zone. Early morning and late evening. S, M, S messages. Damage trust and violate TCP. 8. Timing requirements Mistake number 4. Never switching channels when engagement drops After 2-3 unopened emails, build a condition that routes the contact to SMS as the primary channel. Continuing to send ignored emails is not persistence. It is inefficiency. Mistake number 5. Messaging converted contacts with nurture content Always check for conversion before final push touchpoints and remove converters from the sequence immediately. Mistake number six. Sending too many messages across both channels. Maximum one email plus one SMS per day. And only when both channels serve a distinct purpose at that touchpoint. More than that crosses from coordination into overwhelm. Mistake number seven. Building multichannel without a written sequence plan Decisions made inside GoHighLevel during the build are reactive rather than strategic. Complete the multichannel workflow design blueprint before opening GHL and your builds will be faster, cleaner, and more effective. Multichannel done right feels coordinated and genuinely helpful. Multi-channel done wrong feels like spam arriving from two directions simultaneously. The difference is intention, design, and the guardrails you put in place before you publish. Let's lock in what you learned in this lesson. Multi-channel workflows combining email and SMS generate 287% higher purchase rates than single-channel. because different contacts respond to different channels and a coordinated sequence reaches both groups. Email educates and nurtures. It handles long-form content, rich media, and detailed offers that need space and can wait. SMS activates and reminds. It handles time-sensitive moments with a near-instant 98% open rate. Use the channel selection framework to assign channels by touchpoint. Default to email, add SMS when urgency matters, use both channels together for final push moments, and shift to SMS as primary when email engagement drops. Multi-channel sequence design coordinates channels deliberately. SMS is often used to drive email opens rather than replace the email entirely. And the first SMS should arrive after at least 1-2 days of email-only contact. Behavior-based channel switching uses if-slash-else conditions to detect email non-engagement and automatically shift to SMS. Intelligent adaptation to contact behavior outperforms rigid linear sequences. An SMS compliance under TCPA requires explicit written opt-in before sending any marketing text. Fines reach $1,500 per unsolicited message. Always verify opt-in status before any contact enters an SMS workflow. Coming up next is Lesson 7.9, Testing and Troubleshooting Workflows. You now know how to design and build powerful multi-channel workflows. The next lesson teaches you how to make sure they actually work before they go live with real contacts and real money on the line. You will learn go-high-levels workflow testing tools, how to read execution logs, how to diagnose the most common workflow failures, and a systematic troubleshooting process that catches problems before they cause conversions. Building great workflows is only half the job. Testing them correctly is what separates professionals from amateurs. Let's continue to Lesson 7.9.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a screenshot of an educational presentation slide titled "Lesson 7.8: Multi-Channel Workflows." The title is prominently displayed in bold black text at the top left, accompanied by a subtitle that reads, "Reach every contact through the channel they actually respond to — automatically."

**Key Points:**

* **What You'll Learn:** A list of six bullet points outlining the topics covered in the lesson:
	+ Multi-Channel Performance: Why data & psychology favor mixing channels
	+ Strengths & Limitations: What Email vs. SMS does best
	+ Channel Selection Framework: Deciding the right channel for each touchpoint
	+ Sequence Design: Mastering channel order and timing logic
	+ Behavior-Based Switching: Conditions to switch channels automatically
	+ Compliance: TCPA and requirements for combining Email & SMS
* **Visual Aids:** A diagram on the right side of the slide illustrates the concept of behavior-based switching between email and SMS channels. The diagram features two circles with icons representing email and SMS, connected by arrows and a diamond-shaped decision point labeled "Behavior-Based Switching."
* **Timing and Module Information:** A small gray box at the bottom left corner indicates that the lesson will take 14 minutes to complete. In the bottom right corner, the text "Module 7 • Automation" provides context for the lesson.

In summary, this presentation slide introduces the topic of multi-channel workflows, highlighting the key takeaways and providing a visual representation of the concept. The slide effectively sets the stage for a comprehensive lesson on optimizing communication channels for effective customer engagement.

The image presents a slide from a presentation titled "7 Multi-Channel Mistakes That Damage Engagement." The title is displayed in black text at the top, with the subtitle "Avoid these common pitfalls before activating any multi-channel workflow" written below it. The slide features two columns: one highlighting common mistakes and the other providing solutions.

**Mistakes:**

* Sending same message by email & SMS simultaneously
* Adding SMS to a sequence with no opt-in verification
* Sending SMS at the wrong time (too early or late)
* Never switching channels for non-responders
* Messaging converted contacts with nurture content
* Sending too many messages (overwhelming)
* Building a multichannel without a written plan

**Fixes:**

* Differentiate: SMS reinforces or drives action on the email
* Add explicit opt-in in check condition before final SMS
* Use G2X, send SMS within 10-min post-local time
* Switch to SMS primary after 2+ unanswered emails
* Check conversion before final push, and immediately
* Max one email + one SMS per day with distinct purposes
* Complete the Multi-Channel Design Template first

At the bottom of the slide, a black box contains yellow text that reads: "Multi-channel done right feels coordinated and helpful. Done wrong, it feels like spam from two directions." The background of the slide is white, with a green bar at the top. In the top-right corner, the word "Libre" is displayed in green text.

The image presents a concise summary of key takeaways from a lesson, likely related to marketing or sales strategies. The main points are:

• **Higher Purchase Rates**: 
  • Multi-channel email + SMS generates 237% higher purchase rates by matching channel preferences.

• **Distinct Roles**: 
  • Email educates/nurtures while SMS activates/closings with near-instant 98% open rates.

• **Selection Framework**: 
  • Default to email, then send SMS for urgency, reminders, or re-engaging non-openers.

• **Coordinated Design**: 
  • Sequence design should coordinate SMS to bridge email opens, typically starting after 1-2 days.

• **Behavior-Based Switching**: 
  • Automation adapts via if/else conditions to route non-responders to SMS as the primary channel.

• **Strict Compliance**: 
  • TCPA requires explicit written opt-in for SMS with verification to avoid fines up to $1,500 per message.

The image provides a clear and organized overview of the key takeaways from the lesson, highlighting the importance of using multiple channels, understanding the distinct roles of email and SMS, and ensuring compliance with regulations.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the importance of multi-channel workflows in reaching contacts through their preferred communication channels. The title, "Why Multi-Channel Workflows Win," sets the tone for the presentation, which highlights the limitations of relying on a single channel and the benefits of using multiple channels.

* **Email Only**
	+ Average email open rate: 20 to 30 percent
	+ 10 to 80 percent of your new leads never see the email
	+ Interested contacts who prefer SMS are permanently missed
	+ No feedback when engagement drops after email fatigue
	+ Result: You reach only the fraction who prefer email
* **Email Plus SMS**
	+ Email reaches the 20-30% who open and read
	+ SMS reaches additional contacts – 80% open rate
	+ Together they cover the full spectrum of preferences
	+ Behavior-based switching ensures no contact is missed
	+ Multi-channel campaigns generate 287% higher purchase rates than single-channel
* **Break Message Gets Through Regardless of Preference**
	+ Not every contact prefers the same channel. Multi-channel ensures your message gets through.

In summary, the image effectively illustrates the advantages of adopting a multi-channel approach to workflow management. By leveraging both email and SMS, businesses can significantly increase their reach and engagement with contacts, ultimately leading to higher purchase rates. The statistics presented support the importance of considering multiple channels to ensure that messages are delivered effectively, regardless of individual preferences.

The image presents a comparison between email and SMS as marketing channels, highlighting their strengths and limitations. The main points are:

*   **Email and SMS – What Each Channel Does Best**
    *   Use each channel for what it does uniquely well – not interchangeably
*   **Email**
    *   **Strengths:**
        *   20-35% Open Rate
        *   Long-form content - education & storytelling
        *   Rich media, branded templates & design
        *   Easy to do A/B tests & measure
        *   Can do re-engagement campaigns
        *   Ideal for long-term offers
    *   **Limitations:**
        *   Costly - may get lost in inbox
        *   Spam filters
        *   Demographic changes (e.g., Gmail filtering)
    *   **Best for:**
        *   Nurture Sequences
        *   Education
        *   Detailed Offers
        *   Observing
        *   Case Studies
*   **SMS**
    *   **Strengths:**
        *   95% Open Rate
        *   New customer delivery & receipt
        *   Personal - feels like a text from a friend & family
        *   Short and sweet
        *   High response rate – more than emails
        *   Cut through – no spam filters
    *   **Limitations:**
        *   Limited to 160 characters per message
        *   Requires explicit written opt-in consent
        *   Can be intrusive
    *   **Best for:**
        *   Reminders
        *   Confirmations
        *   Urgent CTAs
        *   Final Push
        *   Re-engagement

The image effectively illustrates the unique advantages and disadvantages of email and SMS marketing channels, providing guidance on when to use each channel for optimal results.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to selecting the appropriate communication channel for various touchpoints in a customer journey. The title, "Choosing the Right Channel for Each Touchpoint," sets the tone for the informative content that follows.

**Key Questions**

The top section of the image poses two crucial questions to determine the suitable channel:

* Does the message need to be read immediately?
* Does the message require explanation or detail?

These questions are accompanied by decision trees that help users decide between email, SMS, or both channels. For instance, if a message needs to be read immediately and does not require explanation or detail, SMS is recommended.

**Touchpoint Types and Recommended Channels**

The lower section of the image provides a table outlining different touchpoint types and their corresponding recommended channels:

* **Welcome and lead nurturing delivery**: Email
* **Educational nurture content**: Both channels (email and SMS) for maximum show-up rate
* **Appointment reminders**: 
  - 2 hours before: Both channels
  - 1 hour before: SMS only
* **Appointment reminders**: 
  - 1 hour before: Short, direct message; no need for email at this point
* **Final offer deadline last chance**: Full message in email; urgent boost in SMS
* **Re-engaging a contact who has stopped opening emails**: Switch channels to break the non-engagement pattern

**Default Channel and Additions**

At the bottom of the image, a note indicates that email is the default channel, with SMS added when:

* Urgency matters
* Immediate reminders are necessary
* Email stops working

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise framework for choosing the most effective communication channel for each stage of the customer journey. By considering the urgency and complexity of the message, businesses can optimize their communication strategy to improve engagement and response rates.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to designing a multi-channel sequence, emphasizing the importance of timing and channel coordination to create a cohesive or intrusive experience for customers. The title, "Designing Your Multi-Channel Sequence," sets the tone for the infographic, which is divided into five sections, each representing a day in a sequence.

* **Day 1: Welcome Email**
	+ Delivers a test and introduces the brand, setting expectations
	+ No offer
	+ Sent immediately
* **Day 1: Value Email**
	+ Provides something genuinely useful, builds credibility, and raises awareness
	+ Sent within 24 hours
* **Day 2: Engagement SMS**
	+ Checks in to ensure the customer received the welcome email
	+ A prompt to read
* **Day 3: Trust Email**
	+ Provides proof that the sequence works for some people like the recipient
	+ Sent within 72 hours
* **Day X: Soft Offer & Nudge**
	+ Sends a soft offer or nudge to push the lead forward
	+ Sent later

The infographic highlights key principles:

* The text in a multi-channel sequence is not a separate message; it's often a nudge to read the email.
* Coordinating channels multiplies open rates.

In summary, the image provides a structured approach to creating a multi-channel sequence, outlining the type of message to send, its purpose, and the optimal timing for each day. By following this sequence, businesses can effectively engage with their customers and increase the likelihood of conversion.

The image presents a slide from a presentation on designing a multi-channel sequence, titled "Designing Your Multi-Channel Sequence" in black text at the top. The subtitle, "The order and timing of channels determines whether your sequence feels coordinated or intrusive," is written in smaller gray text below the title.

In the top-right corner, the word "Libre" appears in green text. The main content of the slide features a flowchart illustrating a sequence of five numbered steps, each represented by a circle with a different color and accompanied by a brief description. The steps are:

*   **Step 1: Welcome Email** (blue circle)
    *   Deliver best initial message. Introduce to the brand's warm and educational tone = no offer (within 15 minutes)
*   **Step 2: Day 1 Value Email** (light blue circle)
    *   Send something genuinely useful, build credibility, no pitch, <24 hours
*   **Step 3: Engagement SMS** (orange circle)
    *   Quick check-in = "Hey, do you have a chance to read [X]? Here's a quick tip."
*   **Step 4: Day 3 Trust Email** (purple circle)
    *   Educate to help – prove that this works for someone like the person (> 72 hours)
*   **Step 5: Soft Offer & Nudge** (lavender circle)
    *   Send a nudge = "We've got a [X] in Stock. Your turn!" < 24 hours

A key principle is highlighted at the bottom of the slide: "The text in a multi-channel sequence is not a separate message – it's often a nudge to read the email. Coordinating channels multiplies open rates."

The background of the slide is white, with a green bar at the top and a dark gray bar at the bottom.

The image presents a slide from a presentation on "Behavior-Based Channel Switching" in the context of Libre, likely a customer service or communication platform. The slide is divided into sections that compare and contrast different approaches to channel switching, highlighting the problems with traditional methods and introducing a solution based on behavior.

*   **The Problem**
    *   A static routing matrix can remain in one channel
    *   Without switching, issues get escalated
    *   The relationship quality dies
*   **The Solution: Behavior-Based Channel Switching**
    *   An if/else condition decides the non-engagement and automatically shifts the contact to a channel that they are required to use
*   **Channel Switching Approaches**
    *   **Email → Live Switch**
        *   **Trigger:** 2+ emails
        *   **Condition:** STAGE 1 - Escalated > 3d; STAGE 2 - Escalated > 6h
        *   **Action:** Live Agent with SME as primary handoff
        *   **Payoffs:** Customers are engaged with agents on a different channel with similar expertise
    *   **Live Agent → Email Open**
        *   **Trigger:** Channel shift
        *   **Condition:** Agent asked > 3h; STAGE 1 - Escalated > 6h; STAGE 2 - Escalated > 3h
        *   **Action:** Send Email - I just want something
        *   **Payoffs:** Save agent on a live channel while giving customers the option to open an email channel
    *   **Contact Removes**
        *   **Trigger:** Like/Unlike on the conversion intent
        *   **Condition:** C2A → Instructing
        *   **Action:** Like/Unlike clicked → YES
        *   **Payoffs:** Handoff to agent sequence, apply conversion flow
*   **Channel Switching is Not a Failure**
    *   Channel switching is not a failure - it's intelligent adaptation. A system that adapts to contact behavior outperforms a system that sends the same sequence to everyone regardless of what they do.

The slide effectively illustrates the limitations of traditional channel switching methods and presents a more adaptive approach based on customer behavior, emphasizing the benefits of intelligent adaptation in improving customer engagement and outcomes.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to compliance requirements for email and SMS marketing channels, emphasizing the importance of adhering to strict rules to avoid penalties. The infographic is divided into two main sections: "Email Compliance" and "SMS Compliance," each outlining essential guidelines for businesses to follow.

**Email Compliance**

*   **Clear Subject Line**
*   **No Misleading or Deceptive Information**
*   **Clear Sender Identification & Physical Address**
*   **Visible & Functional Unsubscribe Link**
*   **Honor Unsubscribe Requests Promptly**
*   **SPF, DKIM, DMARC: Set up Authentication Headers to Verify Sender Identity and Confirm That What You're Sending Is What You Claim to Be Sending**

**SMS Compliance**

*   **TCPA: Obtain Prior Express Written Consent**
*   **Identify Business & Include Opt-Out**
*   **Honor Opt-Outs Immediately**
*   **Time Restrictions**
*   **Short Code: Short Codes Have Stringent Requirements; Short Codes Are Not Automatically Approved; If You Have TCPA Held Consent in TCPA Form or Consent**

The infographic also includes a warning at the bottom, stating that non-compliance with TCPA rules can result in fines of up to $500 per unwarranted text message. This serves as a reminder of the potential consequences of non-compliance and encourages businesses to prioritize adherence to these regulations. Overall, the image provides a clear and concise overview of the key compliance requirements for email and SMS marketing, helping businesses navigate the complex landscape of digital communication regulations.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to compliance requirements for both email and SMS marketing channels. The title, "Compliance: What You Must Know Before Sending," sets the tone for the informative content that follows.

**Key Takeaways:**

* **Multi-Channel Marketing:** The image emphasizes the importance of understanding compliance rules for both email and SMS channels.
* **Email Compliance:**
	+ **Sender Authentication:** No display name spoofing
	+ **Identification:** Clear sender identification and physical address
	+ **Unsubscribe Link:** Visible and functional unsubscribe link
	+ **Response Promptness:** Honor unsubscribe requests promptly
	+ **SPF and DKIM:** Implement authentication headers to ensure email and sender information are properly configured
* **SMS Compliance:**
	+ **Opt-in Requirement:** Explicit written opt-in required
	+ **Business Identification:** Identify business and include opt-out instructions
	+ **Unsubscribe Link:** Honor opt-outs immediately
	+ **Time Restrictions:** Time restrictions must be met
	+ **Short Code:** Short codes are necessary to automatically fulfill TCPA opt-out requests

**Important Warning:**

* Non-compliance with TCPA fines can reach $1,500 per unauthorized text message.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise overview of the essential compliance requirements for email and SMS marketing channels, highlighting the importance of adhering to these regulations to avoid significant fines.
